items.myth: Mutual information measures causal influence from X to Y.
items.reality: I(X; Y) is symmetric and measures any statistical dependence, whether X -> Y, Y -> X, or a common cause Z. Causal direction requires additional assumptions or experiments.
items.example: Umbrella sales and rainfall have high MI in observational data, but I(rain; umbrellas) does not by itself say rain causes umbrella purchases vs reverse causation.

items.myth: I(X; Y) can be negative when variables disagree.
items.reality: Mutual information is always non-negative. Negative pointwise PMI at some (x, y) pairs is allowed, but the weighted average I(X; Y) >= 0 by the Gibbs inequality / KL non-negativity.
items.example: Rare joint events can have PMI(x; y) < 0, yet for fair independent coins I(X; Y) = 0, not negative.

items.myth: Zero correlation always means zero mutual information.
items.reality: Independence implies both zero correlation (for finite second moments) and zero MI, but zero Pearson correlation only guarantees zero MI for jointly Gaussian variables—not in general.
items.example: Let X be uniform on {-1, 0, 1} and Y = X^2. Corr(X, Y) = 0 but I(X; Y) > 0 because Y determines |X|.
